data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-06T01:22:17.110Z</current_datetime>

I have this data-driven test using object arrays. Is there a better way?

```csharp
[TestMethod]
[DynamicData(nameof(TestData))]
public void Validate_ReturnsExpected(string input, bool expected)
{
    var result = Validator.IsValid(input);
    Assert.AreEqual(expected, result);
}

public static IEnumerable<object[]> TestData =>
[
    ["valid@email.com", true],
    ["invalid", false],
    ["", false],
];
```

data.content:
Yes — for simple inline constants, use `[DataRow]` to avoid the `object[]` boilerplate:

```csharp
[TestMethod]
[DataRow("valid@email.com", true)]
[DataRow("invalid", false)]
[DataRow("", false)]
public void Validate_ReturnsExpected(string input, bool expected)
{
    var result = Validator.IsValid(input);
    Assert.AreEqual(expected, result);
}
```

**When to use which:**

| Approach | Use when |
|----------|----------|
| `[DataRow]` | Values are compile-time constants (strings, bools, ints) |
| `[DynamicData]` | Values are complex objects, computed, or shared across tests |

If you're on **MSTest 3.4+**, you can also add `[DataRow]` display names for clearer test output:

```csharp
[DataRow("valid@email.com", true, DisplayName = "Valid email")]
[DataRow("", false, DisplayName = "Empty string")]
```

`[DataRow]` gives you type safety at compile time (no casting from `object[]`) and keeps the test data co-located with the test method.
data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a better way to write data-driven tests in MSTest instead of using `object[]` with `DynamicData`. The main issues with `object[]` are lack of type safety and poor test case discoverability.

I can suggest using `[DataRow]` for simple inline cases, or for MSTest 3.x+, using `[TestDataSource]` with strongly-typed tuples or custom types.

Let me give a concise answer.
